Generalised linear models with R

A short introduction for a 1–2 hour practical

O Rodriguez de Rivera Ortega, PhD

University of Exeter

1 Session overview

1.1 Learning outcomes

By the end of this short practical, students should be able to:

  • explain why ordinary linear regression is not suitable for binary or count responses;
  • identify the three main parts of a GLM: random component, linear predictor and link function;
  • fit a GLM in R using glm();
  • interpret model coefficients on the link scale and the response scale;
  • obtain predictions, confidence intervals and simple diagnostic plots.

2 Setup

2.1 Packages used

Run once before the session if packages are not installed.

install.packages(c("tidyverse", "palmerpenguins", "MASS", "pROC"))

The main examples use package or built-in datasets, so the session does not depend on fragile online CSV links.

library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)

theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

3 From linear models to GLMs

3.1 Why do we need GLMs?

The ordinary linear model is useful when the response is approximately continuous and Normally distributed:

\[Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{1i} + \cdots + \beta_p x_{pi} + \varepsilon_i, \qquad \varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2).\]

However, many scientific responses are not continuous Normal variables:

  • binary data: presence/absence, alive/dead, yes/no;
  • counts: number of individuals, disease cases, defects, events;
  • positive skewed data: waiting times, biomass, concentrations.

Problems with using lm() for these responses:

  • predictions can fall outside the possible range;
  • the variance often changes with the mean;
  • the Normal error assumption is inappropriate.

3.2 What is a GLM?

A GLM has three ingredients.

  1. Random component: a distribution for the response, e.g. Binomial or Poisson.
  2. Linear predictor:

\[\eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{1i} + \cdots + \beta_p x_{pi}.\]

  1. Link function: a function connecting the mean response to the linear predictor:

\[g(\mu_i) = \eta_i, \qquad \mu_i = E(Y_i).\]

The link lets us keep a linear model structure while respecting the range of the response.

3.3 Common GLMs

Response type Distribution Link R family
Continuous Normal Normal Identity gaussian()
Binary 0/1 Binomial Logit binomial()
Counts Poisson Log poisson()
Counts with overdispersion Negative binomial Log MASS::glm.nb()

3.4 The glm() function

The syntax is very similar to lm():

glm(response ~ predictor1 + predictor2,
    family = binomial(link = "logit"),
    data = my_data)

Important arguments:

  • formula: model structure, as in lm();
  • family: distribution and link;
  • data: data frame containing the variables.

Important functions:

summary(model)          # coefficient table and model fit
coef(model)             # estimated coefficients
fitted(model)           # fitted means on the response scale
predict(model)          # predictions on the link scale by default
predict(model, type = "response")
residuals(model, type = "pearson")
AIC(model)

4 A Gaussian GLM example

4.1 Clean the penguin data

Before moving to binary and count responses, we can show that a Gaussian GLM with identity link is equivalent to a linear model.

penguins_clean <- penguins %>%
  drop_na(species, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, sex)

glimpse(penguins_clean)
## Rows: 333
## Columns: 8
## $ species           <fct> Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adel…
## $ island            <fct> Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgerse…
## $ bill_length_mm    <dbl> 39.1, 39.5, 40.3, 36.7, 39.3, 38.9, 39.2, 41.1, 38.6…
## $ bill_depth_mm     <dbl> 18.7, 17.4, 18.0, 19.3, 20.6, 17.8, 19.6, 17.6, 21.2…
## $ flipper_length_mm <int> 181, 186, 195, 193, 190, 181, 195, 182, 191, 198, 18…
## $ body_mass_g       <int> 3750, 3800, 3250, 3450, 3650, 3625, 4675, 3200, 3800…
## $ sex               <fct> male, female, female, female, male, female, male, fe…
## $ year              <int> 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007…

4.2 Plot the relationship

ggplot(penguins_clean,
       aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, colour = species)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "A continuous response: body mass",
    x = "Flipper length (mm)",
    y = "Body mass (g)",
    colour = "Species"
  )

4.3 Fit lm() and glm()

lm_fit <- lm(body_mass_g ~ flipper_length_mm + species,
             data = penguins_clean)

gaussian_glm <- glm(body_mass_g ~ flipper_length_mm + species,
                    family = gaussian(link = "identity"),
                    data = penguins_clean)

round(coef(lm_fit), 3)
##       (Intercept) flipper_length_mm  speciesChinstrap     speciesGentoo 
##         -4013.179            40.606          -205.375           284.524
round(coef(gaussian_glm), 3)
##       (Intercept) flipper_length_mm  speciesChinstrap     speciesGentoo 
##         -4013.179            40.606          -205.375           284.524

The estimates are the same because lm() is a special case of a GLM.

4.4 Model summary

summary(gaussian_glm)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = body_mass_g ~ flipper_length_mm + species, family = gaussian(link = "identity"), 
##     data = penguins_clean)
## 
## Coefficients:
##                   Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)       -4013.18     586.25  -6.846 3.74e-11 ***
## flipper_length_mm    40.61       3.08  13.186  < 2e-16 ***
## speciesChinstrap   -205.38      57.57  -3.568 0.000414 ***
## speciesGentoo       284.52      95.43   2.981 0.003083 ** 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for gaussian family taken to be 139340.9)
## 
##     Null deviance: 215259666  on 332  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance:  45843144  on 329  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 4895.3
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 2

For Gaussian data, the residual deviance is the residual sum of squares. For non-Gaussian GLMs, deviance generalises this idea using likelihoods.

5 Interpreting GLM output

5.1 Coefficients and scales

GLM coefficients are estimated on the link scale.

For example:

  • Logistic regression: coefficients are on the log-odds scale.
  • Poisson regression: coefficients are on the log-count or log-rate scale.

To make interpretation easier, we often transform coefficients back to the response scale:

  • Logistic: use plogis() for probabilities or exp() for odds ratios.
  • Poisson: use exp() for multiplicative changes in expected counts or rates.

5.2 Prediction scales

new_penguins <- tibble(
  flipper_length_mm = c(180, 200, 220),
  species = factor("Adelie", levels = levels(penguins_clean$species))
)

predict(gaussian_glm, newdata = new_penguins)                  # link scale
##        1        2        3 
## 3295.931 4108.054 4920.177
predict(gaussian_glm, newdata = new_penguins, type = "response") # response scale
##        1        2        3 
## 3295.931 4108.054 4920.177

For a Gaussian identity-link model, these are the same. For logistic and Poisson models, they are different.

6 Model checking

6.1 Residual plots

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(gaussian_glm)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

For non-Gaussian GLMs, the same plots are useful starting points, but they are not enough. We also check whether the assumed mean–variance relationship is plausible.

6.2 Key checks for GLMs

Ask:

  1. Does the response match the chosen family?
  2. Are the fitted values in a sensible range?
  3. Are there influential observations?
  4. For Poisson models, is there evidence of overdispersion?
  5. Are predictions being interpreted on the correct scale?

7 Practical exercise

7.1 Exercise: choose the family — solution

Scientific question Response Suggested GLM family and link
Does a site contain a species? 0/1 presence
How many trees are in each plot? Count
What is the mass of each animal? Continuous
How many infected individuals out of 30? Successes/trials

7.2 Exercise: fit a simple GLM Use the penguin data and fit a Gaussian GLM for body mass.

# 1. Add bill length as a predictor.
# 2. Compare the model with and without bill length using AIC().
# 3. Predict body mass for a new penguin with:
#    flipper_length_mm = 200, bill_length_mm = 45, species = "Gentoo".

The second model includes bill_length_mm in addition to flipper_length_mm and species. If glm_body_2 has a lower AIC than glm_body_1, this suggests that adding bill length improves the model. The final line gives the expected body mass for a Gentoo penguin with flipper length 200 mm and bill length 45 mm. # Summary

7.3 Take-home messages

  • GLMs extend linear models to non-Normal responses.
  • The family argument defines the response distribution and link.
  • Coefficients are usually on the link scale.
  • Use type = "response" when you want fitted values or predictions in the original units.
  • Always match the model family to the scientific response.
